data.title: ALPEK Is Trading Based On A Low Point In The Cycle, And Is An Opportunity
data.description:
ALPEK (OTCPK:ALPKF) is a Mexican plastics manufacturer with global operations concentrated in Mexico, the US, and South America.

The company focuses on the PET (PTA/PET/rPET) and polystyrene (EPS, PP, Arcel) value chains. It is the largest producer of PET in the Americas and #2 in the World. It also leads in EPS in the Americas.

Like most chemical companies, ALPEK suffers from cyclicality, with operating margins quickly ranging from 4% to 10/12% in a year. It has averaged around 7.5% operating margins across the last three cycles.

The company's financial condition is good, with some level of net debt, but below 2x cycle-average operating income, with a large part maturing near 2030 and at convenient low fixed rates.

The company's stock price is discounting that current low margins, stemming from global excess capacity, will continue indefinitely. However, from a cycle-average perspective, the stock offers a very attractive yield, with a sufficient yield on a current basis. For that reason, I consider the stock a Buy at these prices.

Company intro

North American plastics: ALPEK is a Mexican plastics producer. Its operations are located mostly in Mexico (36% of capacity) and the US (31% of capacity), with Brazil and Oman as other important operations (about 10/15% each) and then smaller global operations (Argentina, UK, Chile, Saudi Arabia).

The company has a capacity of about 7 million tons of polyesters (mainly PTA and PET), making it the largest PTA and PET producer in the Americas and #2 in PET in the World. Polyester chain products make up about 80% of the company's production and revenues and between 75% and 50% of EBITDA.

PTA is the precursor of PET, in turn made out of naphtha (paraxylene to be precise). PET is then used chiefly for plastic containers for food and consumer goods like cleaning products, and more marginally for textile fibers and other uses.

ALPEK also produces polypropylene (PP), used mostly in food packaging, and expanded polystyrene (EPS) and Arcel, used in packaging and construction.

Cycle dynamics: ALPEK's plastics markets suffer from high cyclicality, as can be observed from the company's operating margins and revenues below. Importantly, barring impairment costs (which are not reflected below), the company never reached negative operating margins, not even during the worst periods of the cycle.

The biggest driver of the cycle is the available processing capacity in the industry and in specific regions. This is because the industry operates under cost-plus or margin-per-volume arrangements. ALPEK publishes these margins in its quarterly presentations.

A second driver is the prices of inputs, mainly oil. The price of oil affects the company's revenues via the pass-through of costs. This is evident on the chart below for revenues. However, as seen in the chart above, higher revenues do not necessarily imply higher margins (for example, between 2014 and 2017 revenues where decreasing and margins increasing).

Finally, only a small component of that cycle comes from the demand for plastic products, which is fairly consistent over time, given that the end uses are mostly nondiscretionary products like food and beverages.

Cyclical capital allocation: One aspect I evaluate with cyclical companies is whether they allocate capital counter-cyclically. This implies paying down debt or accumulating cash during positive cycles and deploying it during negative portions of the cycle.

As we can observe below, this has not been the case with ALPEK. The company usually invests more (negative cash from investing) when the cycle is positive (higher operating margins) and retreats when the cycle is negative. This probably has led to overpaying for assets, leading to impairments during the negative cycle (like the example of the Corpus Christi operation in 2023, in the second chart below).

Manageable finances: The company has also taken debt cyclically, which is not great. Debt taken during the upper portion of the cycle to invest in CAPEX can be complicated to repay during the lower portion of the cycle.

Although the company has taken debt cyclically, its debt levels are manageable.

To begin with, more than half of the debt pays a very low interest. ALPEK owes $1.1 billion in long-term notes (maturing in 2029 and 2031), with a very low coupon of 3.25% and 4.25%. The company also owes about $700 million in credit facilities, paying SOFR + 1.5%. ALPEK has about $400 million in cash to cover liquidity needs if necessary.

In addition, the company's interest expense (about $80 million net per year) is very well covered by operating income even during the current downward portion of the cycle ($240 million TTM).

Valuation

Cycle-average method: Like with most cyclical companies, I believe ALPEK's valuation should be made using the average of the earnings the company can generate through the cycle. We can observe below how the company's market cap has behaved very cyclically, too.

Operating margins and revenue: In this case, we need to apply an average operating margin (which, as shown above, has been 7.75% across three cycles for ALPEK), applied to the company's revenues during the middle of the cycle.

This second point is challenging because the company's margins and revenues do not move in tandem. Using the mid-point between 2020 and 2022 gives a good approximation, given that it reflects a very low point and a very high point for oil prices, which in turn drive revenue levels. That would be approximately the current level of $7.5 billion.

Cycle-average yield: These generate an average operating income of $580 million. From that, we need to remove about $80 million in yearly net financial expenses and 30% of taxes on income (using Mexico's rate, given that it's the country where most profits are generated and where the company is headquartered). This leads to a cycle-average net income of $350 million.

Compared to a current market cap of $1.25 billion, this implies a cycle-average earnings yield of 28%, which I consider extremely attractive.

Current earnings yield: Based on the company's current position in the cycle, it can generate about $260 million in operating income or $128 million after interest and taxes. This represents an earnings yield of 10%, which is not bad considering it is one of the low points in the cycle.

Risks

Prolonged cycle: The industry's cycle is currently at a low point, driven by a cooling economy and especially by excess capacity pressure from China. There are limits to the international trade of plastics (like shipping costs, which are rising, or dumping tariffs against China by the US and Mexico), but this excess capacity cycle in China could last longer than expected, especially if the Chinese economy does not recover, or if it keeps adding capacity. This could lead to a lower average operating income over time.

Spin-off: ALPEK is controlled by Alfa S.A.B. (OTCPK:ALFFF), which owns 35% of the shares of the company. I wrote about Alfa recently. Alfa is planning to spin off its controlling share of ALPEK by listing another public company (called ALPEK Controladora) and then giving shares in that company to each Alfa shareholder. This would not create selling pressure on ALPEK's shares (because Alfa shareholders would receive shares in ALPEK Controladora, not ALPEK), but could still generate volatility.

Conclusions

ALPEK is a cyclical business that is currently undergoing a negative portion of its industry cycle. Despite not having the best capital allocation in the past, the company has maintained profitability in previous cyclical downturns. Its balance sheet has debt, but the debt is manageable and mostly yields low interest.

When evaluating the company from a current earnings perspective, its yield is normal, at 10%. This would not be considered fair for a cyclical business with poor capital allocation. However, when we evaluate the company from a cycle-average perspective, it offers a yield close to 30%, which makes this an attractive opportunity.

For that reason, I believe ALPEK is an opportunity at these prices and consider the stock a Buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Qifu Technology: An Underrated Fintech Stock
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Qifu Technology, Inc. (NASDAQ:QFIN) is a Chinese digital lending firm for young urban Chinese professionals and came into my notice last year and honestly, when I looked at its fundamentals, I liked it. The valuation was pretty solid and earnings were decent. Qifu has turned to be a profitable company over the time and I guess it offers pretty decent value and I think it's a buy at the moment.

This Chinese-based credit tech firm utilizes machine learning, artificial intelligence and big data to evaluate the borrowers and help them in their decision-making process.

Its Q2 2024 results showed robust performance since I last checked, its earnings have significantly increased in this quarter. What caught my attention is that QFIN stock has surged significantly over the last year. The stock price has surged by 108% but is still slightly shy of its all-time high. This underrated tech stock offers great value and I would definitely recommend including it in your tech portfolio.

Q2 2024 Highlights

In the second quarter, the company reported total net revenue of RMB 4,160 million, a 6.3% increase compared to the same quarter last year. Their non-GAAP net incomes surged by 23% and were RMB 1,413 million. This significant surged in the earnings could be attributed to the increase in the users, which surged to 53.6 million. Last year, in the same quarter, its user base was around 47.4 million.

Qifu is poised to deliver value to its shareholders not only in-terms of capital gains but also through dividends and stock buybacks. The company announced a semi-annual dividend of $0.60, an increase of 3.4% from the $0.58 earlier. QFIN also spent US$361 million on stock buybacks.

Growth prospects also look pretty solid. According to Qifu, its total addressable market, or specifically the consumer credit-tech market in China in 2021, was around RMB5.2 trillion and is expected to grow with a CAGR of 9.2% between 2021 and 2026. The total Chinese SME credit-tech market in 2021 was RMB 6 trillion, and between the years 2021 and 2026, it's expected to grow at a CAGR of 35.9%. In my opinion, Qifu's growth in the consumer base and earnings is proof of these estimations, which can be seen in its latest earning report.

Peer Comparison

When I look at Qifu Technology and compare it to its peers in consumer finance like FirstCash Holdings (FCFS), SLM Corporation (SLM), OneMain Holdings (OMF), Credit Acceptance (CACC), and Upstart Holdings (UPST), I think QFIN offers better value than all. First of all, QFIN's P/E GAAP (FWD) ratio of 6.44 is lower than all of its peers. This means QFIN is extremely undervalued and offers great growth potential. Even on the trailing P/E basis, QFIN's 8.0 is lower than most of the competitors, and in my opinion, it indicates that the stock is not priced relative to its earning potential.
