QFIN's PEG ratio is another key highlight. Qifu's PEG Non-GAAP (FWD) ratio of 0.35 again reflects that it is undervalued, and you'll pay less for each unit of the earnings, making it an attractive option in the consumer finance sector. Revenue growth is also pretty decent, in my opinion, with a 3-year CAGR of 5.29% and a 5-year CAGR of 19.19%. I think that in a sector where most of its peers are struggling, QFIN's revenue potential reflects its healthy growth, and it is a positive sign of its viability in the long run.

What I like about QFIN is its solid levered FCF margin of 12.62%, which is higher than the majority of its peers. Also, its return on equity of 21.98% further demonstrates operational efficiency, showcasing growth and profitability.

Overall, I believe QFIN offers a solid growth opportunity at a much lower price point, making it a strong buy.

Valuation

QFIN stock has surged significantly in the past one year and the reasons are obvious, its strong fundamentals. Due to this, the market has respond with great enthusiasm, taking the stock to $31.33 with an increase of 108% over the past one year.

In my opinion, QFIN is undervalued based on valuation metrics. It has a P/E GAAP (TTM) ratio of 8.00 which is nearly 40% lower than the sector median and strengthens my bullish case. This, combined with its P/E GAAP(FWD) of 6.25, reflects that the market has not fully recognized QFIN's future earnings potential. I think PEG GAAP (TTM) of 0.31 further indicates that QFIN is priced attractively relative to its growth prospects, with a 44% discount compared to the sector.

The forward EV/EBITDA and forward EV/EBIT ratios show that QFIN is trading low relative to its sector, with values of 4.49 and 4.52, respectively, both more than 65% lower than sector medians. In my opinion, QFIN is delivering strong earnings with efficient capital allocation; hence the ratios speak for themselves. Its price/sales (FWD) ratio of 2.20 is also 25% lower than that of the sector, showing that the market is not adequately pricing its revenue potential.

Finally, the dividend yield of 3.58% is above the sector median, which is a nice bonus if you are an income-focused investor. Given these undervaluation metrics, QFIN is a clear buy, in my opinion, with both growth and income potential.

EPS estimates for the full year are expected to be around $5.28 reflecting a significant growth of 39.14%. Next year's estimate is $5.61 with a modest increase of 6.4%. Similarly, revenue estimates for FY24 are $2.33 billion and with YoY growth of 2.66%. Next year, it is expected to increase at 2.71% to reach $2.39 billion.

Why am I Bullish on QFIN?

So, here is what my verdict on QFIN, yes, it's the right time to buy. Not only its valuation but also the company's business model and macroeconomic changes drive my bullish case.

The company is in the digital lending business, targeting young urban Chinese with white collar corporate jobs. The company is poised to increase its market share and customer base in China, and we can see that efforts in the earnings growth in Q2 and hopefully Q3 and Q4 will follow the same. I think this will boost its FCFs and will help the company to deliver value to its shareholders with strong dividends and stock buybacks.

Also, I think that on the macroeconomic scale, the recent changes in Chinese policies will help the business to take advantage of it.

QFIN's profitability metrics are another reason why I am bullish on the stock. Its EBIT margin of 45.27% and EBITDA margin of 45.69% are more than double the sector median, which shows the operating efficiency of the company, meaning it is efficient in converting revenues into profits. The net income margin of 28.08% is also a standout, outperforming competitors by 25%, highlighting solid profitability in a sector that has extreme volatility. This means that the company is efficient in recovering the cash it lends to people, thus increasing its profits. While the gross profit margin of 60.69% is slightly ahead of the sector, the return on equity (22.12%) and return on total capital (21.46%) are excellent. Despite a slightly lower free cash flow margin, QFIN's profitability makes it a compelling buy, in my opinion.

Risks

Being a digital lending company, QFIN has several risks. Some risks are related to the tech side, but one risk that any lending company (digital or conventional) will have is its borrowers not being able to pay the money borrowed. And especially in a digital lending business where they lend money without any collateral, it is a serious risk. As we have discussed, the company uses AI, ML, and big data to study its customers, but life is still unpredictable, and any change in the customers' lives, like losing a job, etc., could affect Qifu's revenues.

Another threat is that any tech business would have cybersecurity threats, and any breach could be a serious blow in terms of compromising customers' data, etc.

A policy change on the state level is another risk that could affect Qifu's business.

Conclusion

Qifu Technology, in my opinion, is an underrated stock in the consumer finance sector. The company has a strong balance sheet, steady growth, maintains profitability, and offers consistent dividends. It has all the qualities one could ask for in a stock. Though the risks are significant, QFIN, being a solid credit-tech company, knows how to take calculated risks with its robust system. If you want to diversify your portfolio and looking for an attractive investment in the consumer finance sector, QFIN is the best stock for you.

data.title: SMH: Promising, But I'm Waiting For A More Attractive Price
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I recommend holding the VanEck Semiconductor ETF (NASDAQ:SMH). There is no doubt that the semiconductor segment is promising, and should bring companies a strong increase in productivity of between 0.1% and 0.6% per year until 2040, with the automation of 60% to 70% of employee activities.

This expectation is corroborated by the significant investment that large technology companies are making. However, one of the great attractions of an ETF is the reduction of risks specific to each company, and currently, there is no margin of safety even with the diversification that VanEck provides.

VanEck Semiconductor ETF

Before we talk about the investment thesis, I would like to introduce you to the VanEck Semiconductor ETF. The ETF currently invests in 24 stocks of companies that operate in the production and development of semiconductors, as we can see below.

The ETF seeks to replicate the performance of companies in the semiconductor segment, seeking to follow the performance of the MVIS US Listed Semiconductor 25 Index, promoting diversification, liquidity and practicality for investors seeking exposure to the segment.

Productivity Gains With AI

I recently wrote an article about the iShares Semiconductor ETF (SOXX), where I listed how promising the semiconductor segment was based mainly on geopolitical issues, but in this article, I intend to address other topics.

I will bring studies on the productivity gains that AI should promote for large technology companies and how these strategic investments can become great competitive advantages for these companies.

McKinsey recently published studies on the productivity gains that generative AI would promote in the economy. What caught my attention the most was that the company projects that generative AI should enable an increase in productivity of 0.1% to 0.6% per year until 2040.

However, combined with other technologies, this productivity gain could be 0.5% to 3.4% per year until 2040. This would result in the automation of 60% to 70% of workers' activities, requiring training and reallocation. These projections seem very pertinent, as we will see the investments of big tech companies below.

Investments By Large Companies In Semiconductors Have Soared

One of the most assertive ways to analyze whether a trend has the potential to be structural is to check how much capital is being invested in it, and we can see in the graph below how the big technology players are making a lot of capex in semiconductors, especially for infrastructure related to AI.

This trend can also be seen in the evolution of American industrial production when we analyze three sectors: Computer equipment, communications equipment and semiconductors. Let's look at the graph below.

We can see a clear rise in the semiconductor sector post-pandemic, especially after the announcement of the CHIPS Act and the injection of many stimuli for the American semiconductor industry. But with all these great signs, why my skeptical view and recommendation to hold the ETF?

Valuation Has No Safety Margin

One of the great benefits of ETFs is convenience and security, since diversification mitigates the specific risks of each company. However, when we look at the ETF prospectus itself, we see that the P/E and P/B are extremely high, which already indicates that the asset is not cheap.

In my view, one of the best alternatives to analyzing the valuation of a growth company ETF is by analyzing its PEG. To do this, I will use a tool to analyze the ETF's PEG against its own history.

Since the semiconductor market is extremely cyclical, I will use the horizon of the last three years. As we can see, in the last 3 years the ETF has traded at a minimum of 1.4x PEG and a maximum of 3.4x PEG, making an average of 2.4x PEG in the last 3 years.

But as we can see from the chart, it currently trades at a PEG of 2.5x, which implies a 4% downside to return to the historical average of the last 3 years. Based on this, I recommend holding the VanEck Semiconductor ETF since there is no margin of safety for the investment.

Potential Threats To The Thesis

As threats to my skeptical thesis regarding the VanEck Semiconductor ETF, we can say that it is not a consensus, since Seeking Alpha's powerful Quant tools recommend a strong buy, as we can see below.

On the other hand, as threats to an optimistic thesis, we can mention that the VanEck Semiconductor ETF presents greater risks than the iShares Semiconductor ETF, since VanEck has 24 invested companies, while iShares has 30 invested stocks.

The investment thesis is complex since it covers many stocks, and the sector is characterized by being cyclical and expectations regarding the company's results being quite volatile, so the investor should be cautious when conducting their studies.

The Bottom Line

A great way to corroborate a "great promise" of investment is to understand how much is being invested in it. Unlike themes that are falling into oblivion, such as ESG investments, we could see in this article that investments in semiconductors and AI are very heated by large technology companies.

Although I believe this is a promising segment, as an analyst who follows the value investing philosophy, I do not see enough safety margin in the PEG, P/E, and P/B metrics to recommend buying this ETF.

Based on this analysis, I recommend holding the VanEck Semiconductor ETF. In my view, investors should be patient and wait for a more attractive risk/return ratio to invest in this interesting ETF.

data.title: John Wiley & Sons: (Still) Less Compelling Than Treasuries
data.description:
It’s been about four months since I suggested that John Wiley & Sons (NYSE:WLYB) was less compelling than Treasury Notes. Since then, the shares have returned about 28.7% against a gain of about 8% for the S&P 500. My caution has cost me yet again, apparently. The company has posted financial results since, so it’s time to review the name yet again. I’m going to review the latest financials, and also review the valuation. It may be the case that a stock trading for $49.62 is more compelling an investment than when it was trading at $39.14. This all depends on the financial results, and the relative returns you might earn from Treasuries.

Welcome to the thesis statement portion of the program, where I offer you the gist of what I’m thinking so you can relieve yourself of the potential tedium of reading the whole piece. You’re welcome. In spite of the fact that there were some improvements to the financial results, I’m going to remain on the sidelines. If I were deploying new capital, I’d certainly choose 10 Year Treasuries over these shares for a few reasons. First, there were some not so bright financial spots. Second, the valuation is quite high in my view. The last time the shares traded at current levels, they went on to underperform. Given that you can earn more money, more predictably, with Treasury Notes suggests that investment in these shares makes no sense. Additionally, we should remember that along with being more predictable, Treasury Notes offer capital gain potential, too.

Financial Snapshot

In some ways, the most recent financial results were fairly impressive. In spite of a 10.5% drop in sales from the same period last year, operating income swung from a $16.4 million loss last year to a positive $29 million this year. This drove net income swinging from a $92.3 million loss last year to basically breaking even this year. Fully $47.8 million, of this improvement, comes directly from much lower cost of sales. The company also benefited from restructuring charges that were $8.25 million lower, and operating and administrative expenses that were $6.9 million lower. The income statement paints a picture of an impressive turnaround.

It’s not all animated bluebirds and lollipops, though. The capital structure has deteriorated, with long-term debt up to ~$918.55 million, which is a decade high for the firm by a very wide margin. Specifically, the company left FY 2024 with debt of about $774.5, so it’s up about 18.6% over the quarter. Interest expense is up about 12.9% over the same period a year ago, and I think this figure will grow at a faster rate going forward.

Additionally, things look less rosy when we look at the cash flow statement. Cash from operations was a loss of $88.7 million during the most recent quarter, relative to a loss of about $82.3 million during the same period last year.

Things have certainly improved at the company, but in my view, the reality is that “less bad” is still “bad.” For that reason, I’d expect to see a relatively compelling valuation.

The Stock

In the domain of investing, everything is relative, and the future matters more than the past. So, if you buy one stock, you are, by definition, not applying that capital to a host of other stocks or other investment opportunities. Additionally, my excess of caution a few months ago meant that I missed out on some of the gains here. While I could argue that the Notes I did buy offer a level of predictability that these shares lack until I’m blue in the face, there’s no getting around the fact that my caution cost me. That should not taint my analysis now, though. I need to focus on whether the shares are cheap at the moment. In particular, I want to determine whether or not they’re cheap relative to the risks and opportunities present here.

I think it’s worth noting that when I reviewed this name previously, the 10 Year Treasury Note was yielding 4.228% and the dividend on the stock was 3.5% (.728). Today the 10 Year Note sits at 4.057%, and the dividend yield is 2.8% (1.25%). Using the arithmetic skills not so lovingly beaten into me by the good sisters at Holy Spirit School years ago, I have determined that, as the stock has climbed in price, the relative spread between the Treasury and the stock has blown out by 71%. This makes shares far less relatively attractive in my view.

That’s not enough to disqualify the shares, though. If they’re otherwise cheap enough, it might be worth buying the shares. Those who read my stuff regularly know that I apply a few metrics to decide if a stock is trading cheaply enough or not. Some of these are more complex than others. On the simple side, I look at the relationship between price and some measure of economic value like sales, or earnings, and the like. The lower the price per unit of economic value, the better. In particular, I want to see shares trading at a discount both to the overall market and to their own history. On this score, I think it's fair to say, at ~1.49 times sales, the current valuation of John Wiley is rather high. I think it’s worth noting that the last time the shares were trading at current levels, they went on to underperform. Additionally, I think it’s a mistake for the market to pay $1.49 for $1 of future sales for a company that’s still in the red, even if things are improving.

In addition to looking at simple ratios, I want to try to understand what the market is currently "thinking" about a given company's future. In order to do this, I turn to the work of Stephen Penman, particularly his book "Accounting for Value." One of the most interesting ideas expressed in this book is that share price is itself an interesting source of information, and gives us clues about what the market view of the future of the business is. So, if the market’s assumptions are too optimistic, that’s bad.

We get at the assumptions by applying some high school algebra to work out the “g” (growth) variable in a standard finance formula. The greater the expectations, the more risky the investment. According to this approach, the market currently "thinks" that John Wiley & Sons will grow at a rate of about 11.5% from current levels. In my view, that is excessive, and is yet another reason to avoid this stock at current levels.

At the risk of “droning on” about the negative risk premium here, a risk I’m very much willing to take obviously, an investor can earn more income, more predictably, with a Treasury Note than they would with this stock over the next decade. That, as Americans say, “is not nothing.” To those who would argue that the stock may provide a capital gain, I would remind them that Treasury Notes can do the same.

Given all of the above, I think prudence dictates that I remain on the sidelines here. The shares may continue to rise from current levels, but that’s not as relevant to me as the risk that an investor takes on at current levels to achieve those returns. As I’ve written before, the market giveth, the market taketh awayeth.

data.title: Holding With Mr. Cooper: Not A Buy Or Sell
data.description:
Intro

Mr. Cooper Group (NASDAQ:COOP) originates and services mortgages across the country. The stock price has already run up in anticipation of further interest rate cuts by the Federal Reserve Open Market Committee. The summary of economic projections, also known as the "dot plot," shows the Fed looking towards another 50 basis points of interest rate cuts remaining in 2024.

Front-running multiple rate cuts for 2024 and years beyond, the market has already priced in these effects on long-term bonds and mortgage rates available for new home purchases and refinances. I believe the benefits from the macro environment in the foreseeable future have already been priced into Mr. Cooper's stock, and we will see pressure from the tempering of these expectations as well as normalization to historic book value. Seeking Alpha Quant ratings are in line with my analysis, assigning a HOLD to Mr. Cooper at 3.38.

Macro Forces

Mr. Cooper has benefited since the last quarterly results from the anticipation of further rate cuts. I do not see this as a specific advantage for Mr. Cooper, but a tailwind for the entire sector, benefiting their competitors as well.

Among the macro tailwinds of the moment, there are specific challenges brought about from CPRs (conditional prepayment rates) described in the last quarterly earnings call by President Mike Weinbach...

I'd be remiss if I didn't point out that amortization increased by $47 million sequentially, reflecting both portfolio growth and seasonality. For the third quarter, we guide you to expect servicing income to be roughly flat in the $280 million to $300 million range as CPRs will likely hit their seasonal highs for the year.

Looking further ahead, as we all know CPRs are at record low levels today with likely nowhere to go but up. This means that we expect amortization expense will be a headwind in 2025. Also we're expecting Fed rate cuts possibly starting in September to put pressure on our interest income. These are macro forces, which are obviously outside of our control and we just want you to be conscious of them as you work on your models for 2025.

With additional cuts expected to negatively affect interest income, Mr. Cooper is relying on growing its loan portfolio by acquisition and attracting refinance volume as opposed to organic income growth. Investopedia has a very helpful definition of conditional prepayment rates.

A conditional prepayment rate (CPR) is an estimate of the percentage of a loan pool's principal that is likely to be paid off prematurely. The estimate is calculated based on a number of factors, such as historical prepayment rates for previous loans similar to the ones in the pool and future economic outlooks. These calculations are important for investors in evaluating assets like mortgage-backed securities or other securitized bundles of loans.

Historical Book Value And Fundamentals

Mr. Cooper has increased tangible book value 17% year over year to $68.67 according to their last quarterly report. The stock is trading well above the three-year median book value currently. Examining the chart below, one can see the last time this happened was in the wake of the pandemic interest rate cuts and the low-interest rate environment that followed.

Mr. Cooper is a fine business that efficiently manages its loan book, but I do not believe they have any advantage over competitors like Rocket Companies (RKT) or United Wholesale Mortgage (UWMC), larger companies that can take advantage of significant economies of scale. I believe Mr. Cooper is a transactional business and, while competitive with the rest of the industry, does not have any substantial moat to protect itself from customers refinancing with competitors offering better loan terms.

A comparison of the percentage change in price to book value for Mr. Cooper, Rocket Companies, and United Wholesale Mortgage in the chart above reveals all three closely valued together. I believe this shows that Mr. Cooper is fairly valued at current prices when compared to its peers.

Capital Allocation

Mr. Cooper does not currently pay a dividend, so we must use other metrics to assess the management's allocation of capital. In terms of achieving additional shareholder value, the repurchase of shares is the most direct comparison. At $200 million of share repurchases in the last year, Mr. Cooper has returned 3.2% of their own market cap with buybacks.

In the previous 5 years, Mr. Cooper has consistently bought back shares as a means of returning capital to shareholders, going from 91.1 million to 64.48 million shares outstanding. This averages to 5.32 million shares annually and would cost roughly $516 million on average to continue this pace at the current stock price. The run-up in share price has made it more and more difficult to justify continuing the pace of share repurchases.

Interest Income Spread

Most recently, Mr. Cooper announced a new offering of senior notes, borrowing $750 million at 6.5% due in August 2029. When the 30-year mortgage rate was above 7% this was a solid capital allocation move, forming a 50 basis points or more in favor of Mr. Cooper to originate new loans in a way that is accretive to shareholder value.

As expectations of future rate cuts have tempered, so has the investment spread that Mr. Cooper can achieve. According to Nerd Wallet, the current rate for a 30-year mortgage is 6.6%, only 10 basis points above Mr. Cooper's borrow rate and putting a drag on interest income growth.

Mr. Cooper could lower its weighted average cost of capital by issuing new shares and investing that into growth. This would technically be dilutive to shareholders in the short term but would help strengthen the balance sheet further with higher interest income against lower long-term debt.

Risks

If inflation re-accelerates for any reason, Mr. Cooper would struggle to meet expectations for loan originations and could a lower book valuation of its portfolio. Increased inflation would also mean less volume for mortgage refinances, which is a major part of the Mr. Cooper business model.

Mr. Cooper is also engaging in significant share repurchases, which help to boost the share price in the short term. This has been a consistent effort, as detailed in the last quarterly earnings call.

...we continue as we did this quarter to actually repurchase stock. And we've indicated that we'd be repurchasing about $50 million a quarter and we've been a little bit light the last couple of quarters, but we still see value in our stock at where it's trading.

Should the consistent repurchase of shares slow down or stop completely because of lower-than-expected interest income or the need to acquire additional mortgage servicing rights in order to grow revenue, the share price could face downward pressure until the market appreciates the new strategy in capital allocation.

Investment Takeaway

Mr. Cooper is an efficiently run business, bolstered by macro conditions and share repurchases. I believe the industry tailwinds have been aggressively priced into the stock when compared to competition. I rate Mr. Cooper as a Hold at current price levels and would wait for the share price to fall back below tangible book value in the range of $60-65 to start or add to a position.

data.title: Penguin Solutions: A Name Change Won't Help
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Smart Global changed its name to Penguin Solutions (NASDAQ:PENG). I believe the reason for changing the company's name had to do with its determination to double down on its Intelligent Platform Solutions segment, to "solve the complexity of AI infrastructure".

And yet, beyond its name change, I'm not seeing much prospects in this story. This fiscal Q4 2024 quarterly report was met with a miss on the top line and the bottom line, which led to the stock selling off 15% after hours.

What's more, for my part, I see a stock that on the surface looks cheap, at 9x forward non-GAAP EPS, but that when we look slightly deeper, beyond the name change and beyond the low P/E multiple, more questions rapidly surface.

For instance, why are its revenue growth rates so cyclical? And why was Penguin Solutions so eager to repurchase its shares in the first 9 months of fiscal 2024, only to now leave its business materially leveraged and with limited flexibility?

All in all, I believe this stock is a sell at $17 per share.

Rapid Recap

Back in April, I said,

We know that SMART Global divested of its Brazil business in November 2023. But how the business' underlying prospects are progressing is difficult to get much of a sense.

Furthermore, given that the stock is up significantly in the past 6 months, I don't find that this set of results and guidance compel me to chase this stock higher.

Therefore, I remain neutral on SGH stock.

As you can see above, my call to be neutral on this name made a lot of sense. At the time, I simply didn't buy into its future prospects. And now, with its latest results, I'm further convinced that this stock isn't worthwhile keeping.

Penguin Solutions' Near-Term Prospects

Penguin Solutions specializes in providing enterprise solutions for managing the AI infrastructure and high-performance computing.

Basically, they help businesses deploy and manage AI systems, from initial design to ongoing services.

During the earning call, they reminded investors of their 25-year history in computing and how now they have a growing focus on AI.

With over 25 years of experience in high performance computing and more recently AI, we are a trusted partner that enterprises can depend on to navigate this new era of digital transformation.

In the near term, Penguin Solutions is aiming to get exposure to the growing demand for AI infrastructure across industries.

On a positive note, Penguin Solutions expects double-digit y/y growth in fiscal 2025, with revenue driven by AI deployments, growing memory demand, and partnerships like the one with SK Telecom (SKM) (more on its revenue growth rates soon).

Yet, despite their optimistic earnings call, Penguin Solutions faces several challenges. The AI infrastructure market is highly competitive, with rivals offering largely similar hardware solutions.

Given this context, let's now discuss its fundamentals.

Revenue Growth Rates Fail to Impress

A few aspects that we should keep in mind here. Firstly, Penguin Solutions completed the sale of its Brazil business unit in November 2023. This means that although the comparables starting fiscal Q1 are going to be much easier, we must keep in mind the volatility of these revenue growth rates.

Secondly, given that fiscal H1 2024 was so demonstrably negative (including the spin-off business unit), this means that its revenue growth rates would naturally be poised for "some" growth this year.

Then, this takes me to my final point. Penguin Solutions' fiscal 2025 guidance points to 15% y/y revenue growth rates, plus or minus 5%. That's a massive range and echoes my contention.

It's really challenging to get firmly comfortable over what sort of growth business this is. A business that's growing at 20% CAGR can be afforded one sort of multiple, while a business that is growing at 10% CAGR is afforded quite another sort of multiple.

With that in mind, let's now discuss its valuation.

PENG Stock Valuation -- 9x Forward Non-GAAP EPS

As an Inflection investor, I don't like backing businesses with a net debt position. On this front, Penguin Solutions scores poorly, with about $280 million of net debt. In practical terms, this means that including the after-hours sell-off, just over 25% of its market cap is made up of debt.

Accordingly, this means that going forward there are not going to be any realistic capital returns being offered to investors, even if the share price gets particularly cheap.

On top of that, notwithstanding that Penguin Solutions delivered about $39 million of EBITDA, its free cash flow this quarter was negative. More specifically, the $39 million of EBITDA turned into a free cash flow burn of $20 million. That's not a good EBITDA to free cash flow conversion.

So while looking at Penguin Solution's non-GAAP EPS guidance of $1.90 per share at the high end of its fiscal 2025 guidance looks strong, against the $1.25 per share just reported in fiscal 2024, I still remain cautious.

Yes, this would be a tremendous improvement of close to 50% y/y in its EPS. But just how sustainable is this growth?

And what truly confounds matters is that I can easily see the bull case here. The bull case would center on the fact that yes the business is significantly leveraged, but paying 9x forward non-GAAP EPS makes this a cheap stock, particularly for a business that is evidently striving to position itself for the AI opportunity.

On this front, allow me to be clear, a cheap stock isn't always an undervalued stock. And I believe this stock is a sell.

The Bottom Line

While the name change to Penguin Solutions may suggest a new direction, the fundamentals paint a different picture.

Paying 9x forward non-GAAP EPS for a company with inconsistent revenue growth rates, together with negative free cash flow reported this quarter, and significant debt makes me wary of its near-term prospects.

The AI infrastructure play sounds alluring, but the execution and financial stability just aren’t here.

I see this stock as a value trap. At $17 per share, it's a sell in my book. Sometimes, a name change doesn't solve old problems—Penguin Solutions isn't quite ready to fly.

data.title: Calumet: A Compelling Energy Transition Turnaround
data.description:
Buying a value or distressed stock before it transforms into a powerful growth stock is a great way for an investor to generate exceptional returns. Calumet’s value to growth transition offers this type of rare investment return opportunity. Calumet, Inc. (NASDAQ:CLMT) is an over-leveraged oil refiner that seeks to restructure its debt and become a leading producer of Sustainable Airline Fuel “SAF.” SAF is a type of biofuel that has the potential to cut aviation's greenhouse gas emissions by up to 80% compared with traditional jet fuels. If Calumet succeeds with its transition plan, we estimate Calumet could be a five-bagger in the next few years.

On July 10th, Calumet’s transformation geared up when the company converted from a Master Limited Partnership (“MLP”) to a traditional taxable corporation or “C-Corp”. MLPs are publicly traded partnerships, under the 1986 Tax Act, and not investable by most institutional investors. Because MLPs pay a distribution, rather than a dividend, and generate a K1 at year end, retirement plans, nonprofits, mutual funds, and foreign entities cannot own them. By converting to a C-Corp, Calumet can now attract new classes of institutional investors.

The chart below shows that after July 10, CLMT shares dropped from $18.10/share to $9.97/share, on August 5th, before recovering. We believe that MLP focused investors likely dumped their Calumet to comply with their investment mandates. Other shareholders may have panicked and sold their Calumet shares before new traditional institutional buyers like mutual funds and retirement plans started buying Calumet’s shares in early August. Based on the chart below, with its “cup with a handle” and “backing and filling,” we believe that the requisite institutional ownership turnover is now completed and “the buyers are now in control”.

The 18-year chart below shows Calumet traded to $55/unit in April 2007 when oil prices peaked and before the Great Financial Crisis. We believe that as Calumet’s debt restructuring becomes clear and its MaxSAF programming is engaged, not only is there a historical precedent for its units trading to $55, but a similar share price potential could be justifiable by future cash flows.

Immediate Risk and Opportunity:

Critical to CLMT’s turnaround is a prospective Department of Energy “DOE” loan for its Montana Renewable LLC “MRL” refinery, which could be announced this month. Calumet has $2.035 billion high cost in debt and lines of credit and which cost $234.30mm in interest over the trailing 12 months. If the company's restructured interest rate averages 6% on $2 billion, the potential savings could be $114mm in interest savings per year.

Furthermore, Calumet has been refurbishing its other refineries and has been spending about $120mm on capital expenditures annually and those expenses should decline to $60mm per annum based on past guidance. The work on the Shreveport facility is now complete and should be reflected in the coming earnings reports. The combination of interest savings and reduced capital spending could increase free cash flow by as much as $60mm plus $114mm or $176mm per year. At a five multiple of cash flow, at which Calumet currently trades, one could estimate that Calumet’s market capitalization could increase $880mm in the coming year(s) during which time it fully implements its debt restructuring program.

Calumet announced on October 3, 2024 that Stonebriar Commercial Finance, LLC entered into a sale leaseback agreement for $150mm. This transaction aligns its existing debt arrangements to facilitate an “Eligible Capital Event including a loan guarantee from the U.S. Department of Energy (“DOE”)” which we believe suggests that the anticipated DOE transaction is imminent.

"MRL Amended Agreements The existing sale and leaseback agreements between MRL and Stonebriar encompass MRL’s Renewable Diesel Unit, Renewable Hydrogen Plant, and Pretreatment Unit. These agreements generated $400 million of funding to MRL during 2021 and 2022. The agreements have now been amended to allow for their early termination upon the receipt of proceeds from an Eligible Capital Event, including a loan guarantee from the U.S. Department of Energy (“DOE”). There can be no assurance that the Company will be awarded a conditional commitment for a loan guarantee from the DOE or that any DOE-guaranteed loan will be funded. Upon termination, MRL can repurchase all of its assets from Stonebriar for an amount declining with time. If the repurchase occurs on November 1 of this year, it would cost approximately $403 million."

The Stonebriar sale leaseback transaction is structured to pay Calumet Montana Refining $110 million, which will be used to pay down a revolving line of credit, and allows Calumet to buyback all the MRL refinery equipment it sold to Stonebriar. Furthermore, the company will receive the remaining $40 million on a prospective “monetization transaction” that we expect in the first half of 2025. Assuming this current line of credit is 11.5%, then this sale leaseback could reduce interest expense by $110mm X 11.5% = $12.6mm, though its benefit will largely be offset by the rent it will pay. This underscores our confidence that a DOE loan package will soon be announced.

The company hopes to capitalize on the Montana Renewable refinery and raise some equity in 2025, which will allow it to pay down more debt and reduce its interest expenses further. While nothing definitive has been announced on the MRL monetization plan, we believe that MRL might issue stock or equity based on the high-growth prospects of the country’s top SAF producer. Given SAF’s high-growth prospects and MRL’s low-cost producer position secured by the refinery’s geographic location, we anticipate high demand for MRL shares or MRL equity in the future. Based on the successful implementation of its MaxSAF program, we estimate MRL’s future cash flows could drive a market capitalization for the MRL facility of $5-7 bn in three years. These steps will have allowed the company to finance the Max SAF expansion at MRL and pay off its financing debts.

If the market believes our assumptions, then MRL could sell 10% of its equity for $400 million and use those proceeds to pay down debt. Combined with Calumet’s ongoing debt restructuring, reduced capital expenditures, and rapidly growing cash flows from its SAF business, Calumet could potentially have retired all of its $2 billion in high cost debt by 2027. Calumet will own a smaller share of MRL, through its monetization process, but Calumet will have transformed into a conservatively financed diversified renewables refiner with a high growth SAF franchise and a market capitalization in the range of $5-10 billion in the 2027-2029 time frame.

Furthermore, in the fourth quarter, Bank America showed in a slide deck on Calumet, that two passive index funds should buy about 7 mm shares of the stock in November and December.

Montana Renewables and MaxSAF Opportunity:

SAF is a key additive to jet fuel, like ethanol is to gasoline, in that it reduces carbon emissions in jet fuel. SAF should reduce CO2 emissions in a material way as the world transitions to be less reliant on fossil fuels. The DOE is interested in reducing CO2 emissions, and their loan to Calumet will provide material support to grow Calumet as a leading low-cost provider of SAF to the US airline fleet.

“In our Montana/Renewables segment, we continue to see strong demand for our renewable fuel products. We maintain our outlook of strong demand for renewable fuel products, including those we produce at our Montana Renewables facility. We believe demand for renewable fuel products will only continue to grow as a result of the rapid expansion of corporate decarbonization targets, broad sustainability initiatives, and governmental mandates and incentives that have been passed or announced by national, state, and provincial jurisdictions across the globe. In light of the global decarbonization initiatives, forecasts of future availability still fall short of the necessary emissions reductions that would be required to reach established net-zero goals, as adequate supply does not exist yet. For example, our Montana Renewables facility is one of the only facilities in North America capable of SAF production at scale in North America today. We believe that our position as a first-mover makes us the key producer for potential offtake partners to help them reach their announced targets….”

The high SAF growth prospects shown in the charts below reflect the growth potential for MRL.

Calumet is already selling into the commercial fleet, and MRL is the largest SAF producer in the United States. SAF can readily be added to jet fuel and provide greater mileage according to Robert C. Gibbs of Optima Process Systems, Inc, depending on the feed stock.

The charts below show that major airlines are committed to using SAF and those commitments will scale rapidly in the future.

The chart below shows that SAF adoption is in its infancy and robust growth prospects are forecast for decades. In fact, the International Airline Trade Association expects SAF to increase from ~ 0.1% of air fuel supply to ~ 60% over the next 25 years.

Calumet’s MaxSAF expansion business at its MRL facility is the critical driver of the company’s growth story. SAF is critical to the energy transition. By adding SAF to jet fuel, the blend reduces jet fuel’s significant carbon dioxide footprint. To meet 2050 net-zero carbon emissions goals, blending 5% biofuels with traditional transport fuels is one of the steps in the IEA’s Net Zero by 2050 road map. The IEA’s 2050 Roadmap should limit global warming to 1.5 degrees centigrade. SAF represents a tremendous growth opportunity for Calumet, which is currently North America’s leading SAF producer.

Calumet Business Overview:

Calumet has three business segments: Specialty Products and Solutions, Performance Brands, and Montana Renewables, LLC shown in the chart below. Their adjusted cash flows in 2023 were $251mm, $48mm and $30mm, respectively.

Specialty Products and Solutions is based out of Shreveport and is a relatively stable, growing business.

Performance Brands sells premium lubricants and is a smaller but more stable business than Specialty Products and Solutions.

We believe these two segments are fairly valued at 5 times cash flow or ($251mm + $48mm) x 5= $1.5 billion which was the approximate market capitalization of Calumet when it converted to a C-Corp.

Montana Renewables is the growth piece to the Calumet story.

Company Valuation:

To value CLMT shares, we analyze what the MRL facility will be worth when it is producing 18,600 BPD and 25,000 BPD of SAF in 2027 and 2029, respectively.

We then combine that MRL future value with the value of the Specialty Products and Solutions and Performance Brands segments, which we estimate will be worth $2.5 billion based on the continued modest growth and margin expansion of these business, and the effective retirement of nearly $2 billion in high cost debt.

In the spreadsheet below, we estimated the gross profit of the MRL facility based on prices of $2, $2.5, and $3/gallon of SAF less the cost to produce each gallon, which currently is 70 cents per gallon. This nets gross profits of $365,602,600, $506,217,600, and $646,833,600 in 2027.

We then multiply those gross profits by 8, 10, and 12, which is high, but we believe that this SAF business will be seen as a high growth product line. Picking a 10 multiple on $2.5/gal SAF, we estimate the business could be worth $5,062,176,000 and $7,762,003,200 for $3/gal SAF at a 12 multiple of gross profit.

We then assume that 20% of MRL is sold to the public or to an off taker and calculate Calumet's 80% ownership MRL to be $4,049,740,800 and $6,209,602,560 and add the other two divisions we estimated to be worth $2.5 billion. This leads to an estimated market capitalization for CLMT of $6,549,740,800 and $8,709,602,560 in 2027. We then divide by 85,500,000 shares and get prices of $76.61 and $101.87 per share in 2027.

We then calculate a risk-adjusted price for the shares by using a 25% discount rate and a 15% discount rate. The higher discount rate is more appropriate in this early stage of the company’s restructuring. However, as the debt is restructured and retired, and the SAF volumes ramp higher, a 15% discount rate will be more applicable.

These numbers should be considered order of magnitude estimates as there are some aggressive multiple assumptions used on gross profit when it is more conventional to use a cash flow multiple.

Conclusion:

Calumet Inc. appears to be rapidly transforming from a distressed refining entity into a leading producer of SAF. Calumet has converted into a C-Corp, and its shareholder ownership has turned over, and the shares are breaking out.

The Stonebriar sale leaseback announcement suggests the company’s debt restructuring is engaged. Furthermore, the announcement appears to position the company for a Department of Energy loan which should accelerate the debt restructuring and MaxSAF implementation at Montana Renewables.

The DOE loan announcement appears to be a game changer that would allow several large rapid steps to reduce interest expenses and expand its SAF production capacity. As the company starts reducing its debt service through the DOE loan announcement, the company will generate incremental cash to eliminate its highest cost debts. If their baseline cap ex is reduced, this should enhance their cost reduction program. Furthermore, the incremental cashflows from the MaxSAF expansion should generate additional free cash flow to be directed at high-cost debt or growing its MaxSAF program. The DOE loan program office does not have standard packages as each project is unique, its mandate is to provide “attractive debt financing for high-impact, large-scale energy infrastructure projects in the United States”. The DOE LPO program has $305 billion in loan requests currently, and SAF fits their mandate of accelerating high-impact energy investments in the energy transition.

If Calumet maintains its position as a leading low-cost producer of SAF, and the demand and pricing for SAF remain high as we transition away from jet fuel, Calumet could be a high-growth energy transition investment with a market cap of $10 billion this decade. However, the DOE loan approval is the critical catalyst for this stock and could lead to a $30/share price target in the coming weeks.

data.title: Macerich: Old Retail Is Fading
data.description:
The Macerich Company (NYSE:MAC) is a REIT that focuses on malls and shopping centers. Interestingly, while many REITs yield close to short-term interest rates (4.5% to 5%), MAC yields about 3.8% as I write this. As lower rates imply a growth story, I decided to look and see if this might be going on.

I concluded that the signs do not point to likely growth, given the individual struggles to turn the REIT around, while also facing secular headwinds in retail. While the payout ratio is relatively low, I believe today's yield is merely a fair value and deserves no more than a Hold rating.

Financial History

Over the past decade, MAC's results have been disappointing for shareholders, with a record of declining or unstable dividends.

Seen above, revenues for this business were over $1 billion in the first half of the period, and it has not returned to those levels since 2018. Some of this preceded COVID, but the outbreak of the pandemic hastened this process.

Consequently, the dividend has suffered. Between 2014 and 2023, the annual distributions declined from $2.51 per share to $0.68.

More important is the impact to Funds From Operations (and as adjusted after subtracting capex). Both figures show a trend of decline, but there is one interesting subtlety to this.

While the 90% payout ratio is only required for a REIT's GAAP earnings, current cash flows show that dividend distributions are well below that figure when compared to FFO or AFFO in the last few years. Technically speaking, it's something of an improvement and suggests some safety of the dividend.

The balance sheet is okay. There is no significant liquidity here, but the real estate valued at $6.1B reasonably exceeds the $4.5B in long-term debt. Nearly all of that debt is in mortgage notes.

This is an interesting form of financing, not one that I see with every REIT. According to the company, these notes are secured by the real estate themselves (as indicated in the name of the issue) and are non-recourse to MAC as a whole. The interest rates range from low to middle, and the maturities are all within the next decade, staggered through the period.

Overall, the financial results suggest modest (but not deadly) trouble for the company and raise further questions about their ability to grow.

To their credit, MAC has been reducing the debt on their balance sheet, not leveraging up to force growth.

There was a sharp drop in LT debt between 2020 and 2021, and this was met by an increase in shares outstanding. The company raised over $800M in proceeds, similar to the level of debt reduction. This substantial increase in shares outstanding, combined with decreases in FFOs over time, explain why the dividend today is so much lower than a decade ago.

Business Model

MAC primarily manages a portfolio of real estate assets used for retail purposes. Their main focus is malls, shopping centers, and a category they describe as "anchors," which are large, single-tenant sites, such as department stores.

To give an example of their typical tenants, see the table above. They consist of many recognizable, brick-and-mortar retail operators, such as Victoria's Secret (VSCO), Dick's Sporting Goods (DKS), and The Gap (GAP).

This explains why MAC was hurt by the impact of COVID in particular; lockdowns and social distancing reduced operations of their tenants, which complicated their ability to pay rents.

In addition to their owned properties, MAC also owns interests in other retail properties through unconsolidated joint ventures.

MAC does not have a uniform leasing approach, with some parcels enjoying triple-net leases, while many others do not. Consequently, the company itself is exposed to the risk of the operating expenses on their properties. Aside from depreciation and amortization (which is a non-cash expense anyway), this is their largest expense and one of the main drains on cash flow.

While MAC attempts to have a unified, in-house approach to portfolio management, they employ a more decentralized approach to property management, believing that local managers will be better suited to adapt to the property-specific needs and changes in local markets.

Future Outlook

Up to this point, my summary of the business and its history has been bleak. Most of the optimism for it seems to come from management's own vision for the future and its strategy for dramatic improvement of their business model. The biggest point to hit is management's aim to reduce its total debt by $2B.

Through a combination of asset sales and give-backs, they expect to have the means to eliminate the debt and come out with a much simpler portfolio. While eliminating debt is never bad in and of itself, it does speak to shrinkage of the portfolio. In the near term, this likely means that revenue and FFOs will decline. It is possible that cost eliminations with these moves could make FFOs sustainable. As the payout ratio is already well in excess of the dividend, I don't expect that this will result in a cut, but this alone does not paint a clear picture of growth to justify today's yield.

During the Q2 earnings call, CEO Jack Hsieh addressed concerns about the effects of the shrinkage:

I don't think this plan will result in us being non-relevant. I think we have a lot of relevant assets and particularly like relative market position, like you look at just Phoenix for instance and other parts of the Country. But, I'd say that, for us, the plan is going well. We have tremendous flexibility. It's always a tremendous flexibility on different routes for success, different assets, different ways that we want to move forward, different ways of handling givebacks.

The belief, therefore, is that the flexibility provided by this improving balance sheet is what matter in the long run. He went on to add:

What you all want us to be doing is getting after that $2 billion of debt, leasing like crazy like these guys are doing and the properties will kind of speak for themselves.

In the excerpted slide, management also highlighted the rise in leasing activity, with 2023's levels exceeding prior years, even before COVID.

Concurrently, the current lease pipeline points to rises in gross rents. Naturally, this will be offset by the shrinkage of the portfolio and the gross rent of disposed assets, but if this can occur concurrently with de-leveraging, then I expect a financially sounder Macerich, going forward.

I do raise a few eyebrows at the execution, however. Management forecasts the sale of triple-net assets. These leases impart property-related costs to the tenants, reducing risks to the owner. REITs with compelling growth stories (some of which enjoy higher yields than MAC) boast a model of only issuing net leases, often triple. A lack of similar priority is perplexing to me.

That aside, I also question the confidence management has in the types of assets they seek. Much of the portfolio is going to be replaced with assets that they believe will be better aligned with future trends. I think the history of American retail shows why such a task is necessarily difficult.

As the American economy and population grows, so too do total retail sales, with seasonality favoring the fourth quarter of each year. Between the fourth quarters of 2019 and 2023, sales grew from $1441B to $1892B, a 31% increase. Lack of consumerism isn't the problem for brick-and-mortar.

If we look at only e-commerce, sales grew from $178B to $322B. This is an 81% increase in the same period! While overall retail still grew, the telling fact is that e-commerce did return to growth-adjusted, pre-COVID levels. E-commerce therefore has permanently captured these customers from brick-and-mortar, and even the decade prior to COVID showed that a slower version of this trend was in motion.

For this reason, I remain very skeptical that ideal retail assets can be scooped up by most REITs, much less MAC. More likely is that REITs with warehouse properties, which support the logistics of e-commerce, will have long-term tailwinds to support rent growth.

Thus, when it comes to MAC's fair value, I think the 3.8% yield represents the upper end of possibilities. MAC is small enough that careful selection of assets could result in above-average upside, but it does not seem priced to allow for much disappointment.

Conclusion

Macerich is a REIT going through a turnaround. Some details in this story are cause for hope that it will prove to be a lucrative income source for investors. The dividend is well covered by cash flows, and efforts are being made to tidy the balance sheet and remove annoying assets. Yet, this is a company trying to turn itself around with real estate of an ephemeral market: brick-and-mortar retail.

Profitable growth isn't impossible, but the trends suggest that room for error is small, and the low yield prices MAC for success that hasn't been executed yet. Long-term investors may only get a fair price for a great ending, not a mediocre one. Until a proper discount prices in these risks, I can only rate the shares a Hold for now.

data.title: Freedom Holding: About 7x Increase In Market Cap In 5 Years - More To Come
data.description:
My Thesis Update

I'm one of the few analysts covering Freedom Holding (NASDAQ:FRHC) stock here on Seeking Alpha. Surprisingly, this US-registered financial holding company hasn't garnered much attention from a broad range of retail investors yet. However, since its listing on NASDAQ in October 2019, the company has demonstrated significant achievements: as the company celebrates its 5th anniversary this month, we can see that it continues to grow rapidly and diversify its activities regionally and operationally. The positive reaction of the stock price to the company's fundamental growth has made my bullish calls have aged well:

This growth has allowed FRHC to keep reaching new highs, despite various challenges. I believe that the >720% increase in Freedom's market cap since its uplisting is just the beginning as the management has ambitious expansion plans, and yet the company's stock remains relatively undervalued given the growth rates we're witnessing.

Note: performance since October 1, 2024

Therefore, I reaffirm my "Buy" rating for the medium-to-long term.

My Reasoning

Let's first begin by analyzing the latest financials - after that, we'll zoom out to examine the company's long-term trajectory.

Freedom Holding demonstrated quite solid performance in every business line during fiscal Q1 FY2025. As a brokerage business, it had 49 branches in Kazakhstan, Cyprus, and the United States, offering all types of securities brokerage - this segment employed 1,510 people (1,219 permanent and 291 contracts). Freedom EU oversaw European operations, offering securities trading and margin lending; PrimeEx in New York maintained investment banking operations. Freedom Bank KZ - the banking business witnessed a 3% decrease in assets and a 5% decrease in the loan portfolio, while its deposit portfolio increased by 11%. Despite some downturn in the trading portfolio, from the negative revaluation of quasi-governmental bonds, interest income increased as compared to last year. FRHC also showed improvement in the insurance segment: they hired 944 employees, a majority of them full-time, and retained the leading position in the online insurance market in Kazakhstan, with the number of clients growing by over 29.5% in less than a year. Actually, the clients' growth could be seen in every single segment:

As a result of a widened client base, Freedom Holding's total revenue Q1 FY2025 was ~$450.7 million, compared with $316.2 in the same period in 2023 (+42.5% YoY). Financially, this growth was due to a 51% increase in interest income to $226.0 million "attributed to margin loans, customer loans, and trading securities." Insurance underwriting revenues increased by 188% to $129.4 million, due to "the growing number of pension annuity and accident insurance class", the 10-Q specified. Fee and commissions increased 17% YoY to $115.5 million, driven by "growth in brokerage services, partially offset by declines in bank services and other services." FRHC also experienced a $12.5 million net derivative gain (+141%) due to currency swap revaluations. However, it recorded a net loss of $52.1 million from trading securities, which was caused by "losses in the price of Kazakhstan sovereign bonds." As interest expense, payroll, and insurance claims increased, all operating costs climbed to $409.1 million from $231.6 million, so the net income fell to $34.4 million compared to $68.1 million the year prior. I consider it as a one-off headwind, that should be extrapolated to future periods.

Now let's talk about the long-term business trajectory, which is all about integrating all the business units into a single digital ecosystem. When it comes to digital ecosystem improvements, the way I see it, Freedom Holding took another important step with the launch of its SuperApp, a solution that "unifies core financial functions on a single platform, providing enhanced customer experience and customer satisfaction." I think that this move also prepared the way for penetration into Kazakhstan's telecom and media markets via Freedom Telecom and Freedom Media LLP. CEO Timur Turlov expects that the strategic initiatives at Freedom Media will turn the current and next year's losses into profits by 2026. Although they need to invest for growth (about $54 million), this is relatively small compared to the company's market cap and shareholders' capital. Anyway, I believe that these investments should quickly yield results, given the track record of growth projects in the past.

So in my view, the CEO Timur Turlov has tried to build a productive relationship with investors and market participants locally and abroad - the company's efforts with strategic initiatives have driven up its market cap to $6 billion from $801.70 million 5 years ago when they uplisted to NASDAQ. For me, one of the most remarkable aspects of the company's story is that they have managed to expand globally, to new markets like Europe and throughout Central Asia, not just Kazakhstan. Today, the group offers services in 22 nations with the European brokerage unit of Freedom Holding boasting over 300,000 clients to date. A lot of European markets still remain underpenetrated, and so FRHC's strategic expansion, I believe, has room to run.

As the emerging ecosystem expands, the company will most likely not need to spend significant resources on developing this area and will instead use existing resources - I wrote about this in more detail in my previous article:

In recent years, Freedom Holding has significantly diversified its investments beyond financial services, investing over $100 million into promising startups in Kazakhstan such as Choco and Arbuz.kz, as well as acquiring the payment system PayBox (now rebranded as Freedom Pay). So a robust telecom infrastructure looks like an essential tool for the success of such an ambitious digital platform. Therefore, I see the addition of a telecom business in FRHC's ecosystem as a strategic necessity for their vision to develop a comprehensive super app that integrates all of their services. Together with the development of a local streaming platform, this move represents a significant evolution of FRHC's traditional financial services. So if the company, as I anticipate, doesn't need to spend significant funds on the development of this area and instead utilizes existing resources, shareholders will likely see substantial benefits - this strategic approach should positively impact the shareholder value.

A few more words on financials (since we're talking about a financial institution firsthand). Currently, the company's TBV on the balance sheet amounts to $1.052 billion, which is approximately 17% of its market cap. In FY2019, the TBV was just ~$110.4 million, according to Seeking Alpha data, indicating a nine-and-a-half-fold increase over five years. So this growth is reflected in the stock's performance, which has closely tracked this metric. Additionally, the financial statements show that capital adequacy significantly exceeds regulatory standards. As of Q1 FY2025, equity capital amounted to $178.5 million, marking a 32% increase over the year due to the growth of retained earnings during the reporting period. The company's robust financial position and compliance with international regulatory regulations have rewarded it with good ratings from S&P Global Ratings and several of the world's leading indices.

So, based on all of the above, I believe that FRHC's past accomplishments speak for its strong standing and credibility within the financial industry and that Freedom Holding will likely continue on that strong basis in the future.

At the same time, I see that FRHC stock isn't that pricey for the growth it may offer. FRHC is trading at just 17.15x (TTM) earnings, but that figure includes those one-off headwinds I mentioned earlier.

This means that if we assume that the further growth in net profit will not be as much impacted as before, then the forwarding P/E ratio should be 40-50% lower according to my calculations. Taking into account the growth rates of the last quarters, as well as the prospective organic growth from their ecosystem, the stock, therefore, looks quite undervalued today.

Since 2021, the company's net income has grown at a CAGR of 17.82% according to Seeking Alpha Premium data, even though on a TTM basis the FRHC's bottom line has corrected slightly. I believe the company could return to near its long-term growth rate in the next few years. So assuming a PEG of only 1x, I think the fair value per share should be close to $120.4 if my calculations don't lie - that's about a 22.3% upside to the current stock price.

Risks To My Thesis

As I noted last time, if you're considering investing in FRHC stock, you should be aware of some potential risks.

First off, a significant part of the company's business is based in Central Asia, which understandably raises concerns among a large number of Western investors. This geographical focus partly explains why the company has relatively limited coverage among investment banks. If you're looking for a consensus forecast for FRHC's EPS over the next few years, you'll be hard-pressed to find any. At best, you might find opinions from 1-2 analysts, but not from major banks, suggesting that the company remains relatively unknown in the market despite its already impressive market cap and significant operational expansion beyond Central Asia. Nevertheless, geographic risks could continue to put pressure on the valuation multiple.

In addition to its geographical location, Freedom Holding also harbors a macro risk that we should carefully consider before making an investment decision for the stock. One of the macroeconomic threats to FRHC stock is the possibility of a global recession. This could reduce investor resilience and trading activity, which would have a direct impact on the company's commission and fee income. Geopolitical pressures and regulatory reforms in some markets could redress the balance by creating uncertainty. As a result, FRHC's financial results could come under pressure, which will impact its stock price and investor sentiment.

Another major risk to my thesis is FRHC's valuation - it's also important to admit that my calculations could be wrong. This applies both to the estimate of future ESP and to the assumptions about next year's P/E multiple. If next year's multiple is lower than I predicted or if year-on-year EPS growth keeps falling, my valuation conclusions may be too optimistic.

Finally, potential business disruptions and market restrictions pose significant risks. Litigation could distract management from its day-to-day operations and strategic objectives and impair the company's ability to effectively execute its growth plans. Although diversifying the business outside the brokerage industry is a good strategy, the market size in Central Asia is relatively limited. Efforts to expand into European markets could prove very difficult and limit the company's growth potential.

Your Takeaway

Despite all the risks mentioned above, I still believe that Freedom Holding is one of the strongest fintech companies, not only in its core region (Central Asia, mainly Kazakhstan) but also, dare I say it, in the world. Yes, there were some bottom-line difficulties in 2024 due to various one-off events. However, I don't believe that these one-off events will be repeated regularly in the future. In the meantime, FRHC's net income has grown at a CAGR of 17.8% since 2021, which looks very solid. Since 2019, diluted earnings per share have grown from $0.12 to $5.74, which is a rare thing among already profitable fintech companies globally. I think this fundamental business growth fully justifies the rally in FRHC stock that we have seen over the last 5 years post-uplisting. Of course, potential investors need to look to the future and not the past, but Freedom has something to offer here too. I believe that the company's strategic evolution towards a fully-fledged ecosystem of apps for different sectors (fintech, telecom, etc.) will remain a key driver of business growth - the growth of the stock should follow suit. I therefore maintain my "Buy" rating unchanged this time around, expecting over 22% upside in the next 12 months.

Good luck with your investments!

data.title: A Chinese Cautionary Tale For The United States
data.description:
Herb Stein famously said that if something cannot go on forever, it will stop. If ever there was a major country that was in the process of learning this lesson, it has to be China with the bursting of its property and credit market bubble. If ever there was a country that needed to heed this lesson before it was too late, it has to be the United States with its parlous public finances.

China has had the mother of all housing and credit market bubbles. According to the Bank for International Settlements, between 2008 and 2020 Chinese credit to the non-financial private sector increased by around 100 percent of GDP. That represents a larger increase in credit than that which preceded either Japan's lost economic decade in the 1990s or that which preceded the 2008 US housing bust.

Meanwhile, according to Harvard's Kenneth Rogoff, China's housing sector came to represent almost 30 percent of its economy and Chinese housing prices in relation to income in its major cities came to exceed those in London and New York. Further underlining the excesses of China's housing market bubble are estimates that China currently has anywhere between 65 and 90 million unoccupied housing units.

There is now every indication that the Chinese housing and credit market bubble has burst. Most of China's major property developers, including most notably Evergrande and Country Garden, have defaulted on their loans. They have also reneged on their obligations to complete the housing projects for which they had received advance payments. At the same time, over the past year, the value of new homes sold has declined by over 20 percent and property prices have declined by around seven percent.

Like Japan, before it, China will face the greatest of difficulties in resolving its over-indebtedness problem. Moody's is indicating that China's economy will slow to four percent in 2024 rather than the five percent that the Chinese government is projecting. At the same time, China is on the cusp of deflation that threatens to increase the real debt burden. Further casting a dark cloud over China's prospects to grow its way out of its debt problem is the rising tide of protectionism that China is now facing both in the United States and Europe. Underlining this point is Donald Trump's threat to impose a 60 percent tariff on all Chinese imports.

Before we engage in schadenfreude over our rival's economic misfortunes, we might want to ask whether we too might not be on a path to long-term economic misfortune as a result of the parlous state of our public finances.

According to the Congressional Budget Office, we are currently running a budget deficit of around six percent of GDP at a time of close to full employment. At the same time, our public debt is around 100 percent of GDP and on present policies, even before taking into account the lavish campaign promises of both Kamala Harris and Donald Trump, it is on a path to exceed 120 percent of GDP by 2034. That would exceed the debt level at the end of the Second World War. More worrying yet are the debt projections of the Committee for a Responsible Federal Budget. It is projecting that by the mid-2030s, the debt to GDP ratio would rise to 133 percent under a Harris administration and to 145 percent under a Trump administration.

China's current economic misfortunes should serve as a wake-up call to us that high debt levels can lead to serious long-term economic troubles. In our case, it could take the form of a dollar crisis or the return of the bond vigilantes as both foreign and domestic investors lose confidence in our political willingness to address our public finance problems. We have to hope that whoever wins this November's election walks back their lavish campaign spending and tax cut proposals and seeks to build a bipartisan coalition to rectify our public finance mess before it is too late.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
